coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-superparamagnetic nanoparticles capture coupling siderophore-Fe3+ scavenging actuated with carbon dots to confer an “off-on” mechanism for the ultrasensitive detection of Helicobacter pylori
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhuangzhuang
Wang, Hongying
Cheng, Xiaohong
Geng, Jiayue
Wang, Lili
Dong, Quanjiang
Liu, Chenguang
Chi, Zhenming
Chi, Zhe
coredata.dc:description: The detection of Helicobacter pylori infection in human feces is an appropriate non-invasive diagnostic method. However, the antibody-dependent stool antigen immunoassay bears many challenges. Therefore, we developed an antibody-independent biosensing platform. The core of this platform was a triple-module biosensor. The first module was Ca2+-doped superparamagnetic nanoparticles modified with an H. pylori-specific aptamer, functioning to selectively capture H. pylori cells from samples. The second module was a bifunctional co-polymer of chloroprotoporphyrin IX iron (III)-polyethylene glycol-desferrioxamine, which could bind to H. pylori with high affinity and chelate Fe3+ from the third module of Fe3+-quenched carbon dots (CDs) solution. When the formed module 1-H. pylori-module 2 complexes reacted with module 3, a subsequent magnetic separation could scavenge Fe3+, causing fluorescence recovery from quenched CDs as the transducing mechanism. This transducer could respond to tiny changes in Fe3+ concentration with distinguishable fluorescence differences, thus conferring the biosensor with high sensitivity, a wide detection range of 10–107 CFU/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1 CFU/mL. From simulated human stool samples, H. pylori was enriched with a centrifugal microfluidic plate to eliminate any interference from matrices, and the bacteria were subjected to detection using the biosensor. The actual LOD for the biosensing platform coupling microfluidics and the biosensor was 101, and the total time taken was 65 min. This work showcases an instant, accurate, and ultra-sensitive diagnosis of H. pylori in feces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Helicobacter pylori
Feces diagnosis
Aptamer-SPMNPs
Siderophore-Fe3+ scavenging
CD fluorescence Recovery
Ultrasensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of simulated fecal samples and enrichment of bacteria using microfluidics
Characteristics and functions of biosensing modules
Detection of H. pylori in simulated fecal samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Technology Research and Development Program of Shandong

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province [Grant number: 2019GSF107065 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy images of superparamagnetic nanoparticles (SPMNPs). (b) The 16S rDNA tag analysis of bacteria captured using Ca/SPMNPS@Hp1 from the artificial bacterial community. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. abcdef, data with different symbols are significantly different. (c) Photograph of the dark-green powder obtained using resin purification. (d) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of bifunctional NH2-PEG-COOH (black groove), the intermediate product of NH2-PEG-DFO copolymer (red groove), and the dark-green powder (blue groove). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescent images of the targeting ability of Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer to Helicobacter pylori and Escherichia coli observed under confocal laser microscopy (Ex: 488 nm. Em: 519 nm). Image under the bright field (BF) for H. pylori incubated with FL488-Hemin-PEG-DFO (a) and the corresponding image under the fluorescent field (FF) (b), and their merged image (c); Image under BF for E. coli incubated with FL488-Hemin-PEG-DFO (d) and the corresponding image under FF (e), and their merged image (f). Scale bar: 10.0 μm.
(a) The quenching effect of Fe3+ (0–3.0 nM) on carbon dots (CDs) (30.0 ng/mL) solution (Em: 365 nm, Ex: 400 nm–600 nm). (b) Working principle of the triple-module biosensor.

(a) Spectra of recovered fluorescence for the detection of H. pylori at 0, 10, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, and 107 CFU/mL using the biosensor (cyan to purple), and the fluorescence spectrum for the original module 3 as a blank control (black). (b) Linear correlation between the fluorescence recovery ratio and cell densities of H. pylori. F: fluorescence intensity from CDs after any operation; F0: fluorescence intensity when CDs were quenched. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photograph of the centrifugal microfluidic plate (a), operation of the plate in a horizontal centrifuge (b), and its microfluid profile and working principle (c). Results of H. pylori detection in simulated fecal samples using microfluidics coupled with the biosensor (d). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. abcdefgh, data with different symbols are significantly different.

Schematic illustration of the biosensor for detecting Helicobacter pylori; (a) Chemical structure of the three modules of the biosensor. For module 2, hemin and desferrioxamine were linked to heterobifunctional polyethylene glycol (NH2-PEG-COOH) via amide bonds; (b) Proposed mechanism of the designed biosensor for H. pylori.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-superparamagnetic nanoparticles capture coupling siderophore-Fe3+ scavenging actuated with carbon dots to confer an “off-on” mechanism for the ultrasensitive detection of Helicobacter pylori

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of Helicobacter pylori infection in human feces is an appropriate non-invasive diagnostic method. However, the antibody-dependent stool antigen immunoassay bears many challenges. Therefore, we developed an antibody-independent biosensing platform. The core of this platform was a triple-module biosensor. The first module was Ca2+-doped superparamagnetic nanoparticles modified with an H. pylori-specific aptamer, functioning to selectively capture H. pylori cells from samples. The second module was a bifunctional co-polymer of chloroprotoporphyrin IX iron (III)-polyethylene glycol-desferrioxamine, which could bind to H. pylori with high affinity and chelate Fe3+ from the third module of Fe3+-quenched carbon dots (CDs) solution. When the formed module 1-H. pylori-module 2 complexes reacted with module 3, a subsequent magnetic separation could scavenge Fe3+, causing fluorescence recovery from quenched CDs as the transducing mechanism. This transducer could respond to tiny changes in Fe3+ concentration with distinguishable fluorescence differences, thus conferring the biosensor with high sensitivity, a wide detection range of 10–107 CFU/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1 CFU/mL. From simulated human stool samples, H. pylori was enriched with a centrifugal microfluidic plate to eliminate any interference from matrices, and the bacteria were subjected to detection using the biosensor. The actual LOD for the biosensing platform coupling microfluidics and the biosensor was 101, and the total time taken was 65 min. This work showcases an instant, accurate, and ultra-sensitive diagnosis of H. pylori in feces.

ce:section.ce:para: Helicobacter pylori is a spiral-shaped, gram-negative human pathogenic bacterium that infects the gastric mucosa (Chey, 2019). H. pylori infection occurs in 25–50% of the population in developed countries and in approximately 70–90% of the population in developing countries (Cong et al., 2019). H. pylori infection is etiologically associated with chronic gastritis, peptic ulcers, primary gastric B-cell lymphoma, and gastric carcinoma. Thus, an accurate and effective diagnostic method is required to detect the infection (Tonkic et al., 2018), and a robust treatment strategy is required to treat patients (Westmeier et al., 2018). Clinically, the diagnosis of H. pylori depends mainly on the 13C-urea breath test; however, this test can only be performed after at least 4–8 weeks since the completion of drug treatment (Tonkic et al., 2018). Moreover, this approach is contraindicated in children and pregnant women due to radiation exposure (Chen et al., 2019; Tonkic et al., 2018). The serum antibody test and invasive endoscopy are also not suitable for children due to low sensitivity and acceptability (Leal et al., 2008). Considering the above-mentioned disadvantages, H. pylori diagnosis from stool samples is a feasible solution (Chen et al., 2019; Leal et al., 2011). However, the present stool antigen test sometimes gives false negative results in cases of low bacterial abundance and if proton-pump inhibitors or antibiotics have recently been used in patients (Tonkic et al., 2018). An ultrasensitive sensor for whole-cell detection of H. pylori from human fecal samples was developed in 2019. It operates in the “sandwich” mode, and it utilizes H. pylori-monoclonal antibodies conjugated with magnetic beads for capturing bacterial cells and H. pylori-polyclonal antibodies conjugated with fluorescence quantum dot probes for signal transduction (Chen et al., 2019). However, a more cost-effective and sensitive alternative to the use of expensive and time-consuming antibodies is required in the detection of H. pylori. Over the past few years, aptamers have emerged as highly specific binders to a broad diversity of ligands (Munzar et al., 2019). Aptamers are produced via in vitro synthesis without using any organisms, thus providing superior immunogenicity and stability and lower production cost as compared to antibodies (Oelkrug et al., 2015).

For many sensors, the fluorescence sensing methodology is promising due to its high sensitivity (Kim et al., 2016). So far, among the utilized fluorophores, carbon dots (CDs) have become popular due to advantages such as tunable opto/chemical properties, high fluorescence stability, good biocompatibility, low or non-toxicity, and cost-effectiveness, thus underpinning their potential application as bioimaging probes, gene/drug delivery agents, and biosensors and in other chemical or medical fields (Cui et al., 2019; Devi et al., 2019). CDs can function as electron donors and accepters, and their fluorescence can be quenched on interaction with metal ions via electron transfer (Huang et al., 2013; Li et al., 2018). Based on this mechanism, many novel sensors have been developed to detect metal ions (Guo et al., 2015). Currently, most CD-based Fe3+ detection assays have been constructed using the fluorescence turn-off mechanism because Fe3+ can quench the fluorescence of CDs on binding (Guo et al., 2015). Interestingly, when graphene carbon dots (GQDs) are incubated with Fe3+, the iron chelator of EDTA cannot competitively seize Fe3+ to free GQDs from Fe3+ binding due to the strong interactions between Fe3+ and GQDs. Thus, an “off-on” sensor cannot be achieved through the recovery of quenched fluorescence from GQDs (Huang et al., 2013). Nevertheless, we hypothesized that the scavenging of Fe3+ from CDs with a stronger iron chelator can lead to the development of a “turn-on” sensor, which can easily measure weak signals against a “dark” background to reduce false positive signals and increase the sensitivity (Fan and Jones, 2006; Zhu et al., 2015).

Siderophores, produced by bacteria and fungi under iron-depletion conditions, are a group of low molecular weight compounds (400–1000 kDa) with a high affinity to Fe3+, with association constants varying approximately between 1012-1052. Thus, they can chelate extremely low concentrations of Fe3+ from surrounding environments (Lu et al., 2019; Saha et al., 2016). This property can lead to a stronger affinity between siderophore and Fe3+ than that between EDTA and Fe3+, which may enable an effective deprivation of Fe3+ from CD surfaces and consequently lead to the recovery of fluorescence emitted from CDs. Based on this point, a novel “turn-on” actuator with high sensitivity can be developed.

In practice, original samples, such as pathological or food specimens, contain complicated matrices (Chen et al., 2019), which can emit fluorescence and strongly affect the efficacy of fluorescent transducers. Currently, magnetic beads have been widely used to enrich pathogenic bacteria from samples, such as immunomagnetic beads to capture H. pylori from feces (Chen et al., 2019) and Campylobacter jejuni from milk, beverages, and water (He et al., 2018). The binding affinities of aptamers and chemical antibodies are similar (Groff et al., 2015). Moreover, aptamers possess several advantages over antibodies such as broader universality, lower biosensor limitations, and lower cost (Crivianu-Gaita and Thompson, 2016). The utilization of aptamer-modified magnetic beads would allow for easy and rapid separation of analytes from complicated matrices, thus enabling the measurement of a high signal-to-noise ratio (Modh et al., 2018). Therefore, aptamer-modified magnetic beads or magnetic nanoparticles are promising tools to conveniently capture microorganisms from samples. Further, inspired by the typical “sandwich” immunoassays of biosensing (Chen et al., 2019), a second highly specific ligand should be used for mediating the affinity binding between the sensing/actuating modules of sensors and H. pylori. It has been elucidated that gram-negative bacteria, including H. pylori, can circumvent the host iron nutritional immunity by actively transporting and utilizing heme from the host (Richard et al., 2019). This suggests the use of chloroprotoporphyrin IX iron (III) (hemin), a stable and easily available oxidative form of heme in vitro, as a ligand molecule that may endow a specificity to H. pylori.

Based on these data, we conducted a study to develop an antibody-independent “off-on” biosensor, constructed using chemically synthesized elements incorporated with biologically derived molecules, including aptamers, hemin, and siderophores, for the in vitro whole-cell detection of H. pylori. The biosensor was designed to have the following modules (Scheme 1 a): 1) Fe3O4 superparamagnetic nanoparticles modified with H. pylori-specific aptamers (SPMNPs@aptamer) as a bacterial enriching module; 2) a synthesized co-polymer of hemin-polyethylene glycol (PEG5000)-desferrioxamine (Hemin-PEG-DFO) as a bifunctional targeting and actuating module; and 3) the Fe3+ quenched CD solution (Fe3+/CDs) as a transducing module. SPMNPs@aptamer were expected to capture and enrich H. pylori cells from aqueous solutions via magnetic separation. Subsequently, the Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer was anticipated to bind to the captured H. pylori cells via hemin, forming a SPMNPs@aptamer-H. pylori-co-polymer complex. This complex was also expected to chelate ferric ions from the Fe3+/CDs solution, and it turned on the fluorescence of Fe3+-quenched CDs via Fe3+ scavenging on separation using magnetism (Scheme 1b). Upon acquisition of these three modules, the working conditions of this triple-module biosensor were specified in detail. Furthermore, human feces were artificially blended with H. pylori to simulate pathological specimens. To reduce the interference of fecal contents on biosensing, centrifugal microfluidics (Strohmeier et al., 2015) was applied to separate and enrich the bacteria from fecal specimens. The obtained samples were subjected to the above-designed biosensor to testify the detection of H. pylori cells. The coupling of microfluidic plate and biosensor together formed a biosensing platform for the detection of H. pylori. The efficiency of using this microfluidics-assisted biosensing platform for the detection of fecal H. pylori has been presented in detail and its potential in practical use has been described. This biosensing platform is highly potential to overcome the obstacles in the present non-invasive diagnosis for H. pylori.
The materials and methods for synthesizing and characterizing each module of the biosensor and for the absolute quantification of H. pylori are presented in Supplementary Material.

The fluorescence recovery ratio (ΔI) was determined as follows (Whelan et al., 2003):
Δ I =
F −
F 0
F
where F is the fluorescence intensity from CDs after any operation and F0 is the fluorescence intensity when CDs were quenched.
The fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence microplate reader (TECAN Infinite® M200 PRO, Switzerland) under the excitation wavelength at 365 nm emission wavelength at 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of simulated fecal samples and enrichment of bacteria using microfluidics
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 300.0 mg of raw fecal samples from six healthy people, including 3 babies and 3 adults (50.0 mg for each one), was mixed thoroughly and diluted 100 times with 1 × PBS buffer. The diluted solution (1.0 mL) was blended with H. pylori to simulate an infected solution. The final cell density of H. pylori was adjusted dependently. Bacteria within the simulated samples were enriched with centrifugal microfluidic plates (Fig. S1), which comprised a PDMS microchip layer (60 mm × 18 mm × 4 mm) and glass slide layer. The sample chamber (1.0 mL) was at the center of the PDMS microchip. One end of the PDMS microchip comprised a loading chamber, an enrichment channel, and a collection hole (10.0 μL), and the other end comprised a venting channel and venting hole. To implement bacterial enrichment, 1.0 mL of simulated sample solution was separately injected into the sample chamber. After each injection, the microfluidic plate was subjected to horizontal centrifugation at 500 × g for 5.0 min. The collected bacterial solution was resuspended in 1.0 mL of 1 × PBS buffer.

Experiments were conducted in triplicate (n = 3) or sextuplicate (n = 6) as required, and the results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Comparative studies of means were performed using one-way ANOVA. p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and functions of biosensing modules

ce:section.ce:para: SPMNPs are promising tools to capture and separate biomolecules, including bacteria from complex matrices in vitro (Masud et al., 2019); therefore, we prepared SPMNPs in this study. The obtained product was a black powder that could easily disperse in water without aggregation (Fig. S2a), and it had negative surface charges with a Ζ-potential of −25.7 mV. The characteristics of the black powder (Figs. S2b–h) were in close agreement with those of previously reported SPMNPs, confirming it to be the desired water-dispersible SPMNPs.

It has to be noticed that the as-prepared SPMNPs are carrying surficial carboxyl groups (Fig. S2), which would have electrostatic interactions with bacterial cells, such as Lactobacillus reuteri and Escherichia coli (Zhao et al., 2018). This is highly concerning as it might lead to an unselective capture of bacteria via the as-synthesized SPMNPs; thus, they should be investigated. To do so, in 1.0 mL of 1 × PBS buffer, 1.5 mg of SPMNPs were incubated with an artificial community of common gastrointestinal bacteria, including H. pylori, E. coli, Streptococcus gordonii (Badireddy et al., 2014), Lactobacillus johnsonii (Hsieh et al., 2012), Ralstonia pickettii (Udayappan et al., 2017), Shigella dysenteriae (Singh and Barnard, 2017), Lactobacillus casei, Enterococcus faecalis, and Bifidobacterium bifidum (Kim et al., 2018a), each with a cell density of 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL, for 60.0 min, and this was followed by magnetic separation.

Determination of the remaining bacterial density revealed that approximately 33.6% of the bacteria were captured and separated from the suspension using the as-prepared SPMNPs (Fig. S3a, 0% CaCl2). This result indicated there indeed existed non-specificity of SPMNPs towards bacterial cells, in which the electrostatic interactions between the carboxyl groups on as-prepared SPMNPs (Liu et al., 2009) and surface groups on the bacteria (Zhao et al., 2018) might played a key role as concerned above. To reduce or avoid non-specific binding, CaCl2, which can mediate the formation of “calcium bridges” ion bonds between carboxyl groups (Mo et al., 2012), was used to neutralize the surface charges of SPMNPs to reduce their non-specificity. To achieve this, SPMNPs were treated with CaCl2 solution (dissolved in 1 × PBS buffer) at various concentrations (from 0 to 7.0% w/v), and subsequently, they were prepared again via magnetic separation and 1 × PBS washing. As shown in Fig. S3a, when 5.0% (w/v) CaCl2-treated SPMNPs were incubated with the bacterial community under the same conditions as above, the remaining bacterial cell density critically increased to 7.9 × 107. Additionally, the Ζ-potential of CaCl2-incubated SPMNPs changed to −11.4 mV. These results indicated that doped Ca2+ on the surfaces of as-prepared SPMNPs was able to partially block the surface negative charges, thus reducing most of the non-specificity of as-synthesized SPMNPs to the bacterial community. Therefore, Ca2+-doped SPMNPs (Ca/SPMNPs) were applied to all subsequent assays.

Upon acquisition of Ca/SPMNPs, their specificity towards the whole cell of H. pylori was casted via modification with a H. pylori-specific aptamer Hp1, whose selection, characterization, and recognition are specified in the Supplementary Material (Figs. S4a–f). The conjugation between Hp1 and Ca/SPMNPs was validated with EMSA (Fig. S3b). Moreover, the highest grafting ratio of Hp1 to Ca/SPMNPs was determined to be 326.0 ng aptamer/mg Ca/SPMNPs after reaction for 16 h (Figs. S4g and h), resulting in the as-designed enriching module 1 of Ca/SPMNPs@Hp1. The specificity of module 1 to H. pylori was verified by subjecting 1.5 mg/mL of Ca/SPMNPs@Hp1 to the above bacterial community. PCR and sequencing with 16S rDNA tags of the bacteria released from Ca/SPMNPs@Hp1 revealed that this module could specifically capture H. pylori cells from the constructed bacterial community in an overwhelming majority of over 99.0% (Fig. 1 b). This result suggested that the conjugation of Hp1 aptamer on Ca/SPMNPs successfully conferred high H. pylori specificity to these magnetic nanoparticles and eliminated most of their non-specificity. Thus, Ca/SPMNPs@Hp1 were verified to be a highly specific enriching module 1 for H. pylori as initially described in this study.

Upon acquisition of module 1, the Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer, designed as the bifunctional actuating module 2, was synthesized, and a dark green powder was obtained (Fig. 1c). FT-IR analysis was used to characterize the powder using heterobifunctional PEG (NH2-PEG-COOH, MW ≈ 5000), and its characteristics were compared with those of the intermediate product of NH2-PEG-DFO co-polymer. As shown in Fig. 1d, NH2-PEG-DFO spectrum showed new characteristic peaks at 1540.0 cm−1 and 1262.0 cm−1, which could be assigned to –CONH– and C–N bond vibrations, respectively (Hua et al., 2016). The NH2-PEG-COOH spectrum did not show these peaks, suggesting the formation of amide bonds in NH2-PEG-DFO co-polymer. Besides, the NH2-PEG-DFO spectrum showed a small peak at 2310.0 cm−1, which was ascribed to free –OH groups (Lazaridou et al., 2020), while NH2-PEG-COOH did not show this peak. As DFO was the only molecule with free –OH groups, these data, together with amide signals, indicated that DFO was conjugated to NH2-PEG-COOH via amide bonds. Moreover, after reacting NH2-PEG-COOH with hemin and purifying it with an anion exchange resin, the FT-IR spectrum of Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer revealed two distinct spectral regions, namely, Fe coordination bonds and porphyrin at 536.0 cm−1 and 973.0 cm −1, respectively (Rafiei et al., 2017), which indicated the conjugation of hemin to NH2-PEG-COOH co-polymer. Furthermore, XPS survey (Fig. S5) and chemical quantification (Table S1) confirmed that the obtained dark green powder was a simple linear molecule consisting of one molecule of PEG backbone conjugated to one molecule of hemin moiety at one terminal and one molecule of DFO moiety at the other (molar ratio of hemin:PEG:DFO = 1:1:1).

Furthermore, Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer was confirmed to possess a H. pylori-targeting property. It was verified by the assays that, the Alexa Fluor™ 488 labelled Hemin-PEG-DFO (FL488-Hemin-PEG-DFO, Fig. S6) co-polymer could be absorbed to the cell wall of H. pylori to image its profile with bright green fluorescence (Fig. 2 a, b and c), whereas no such result was observed for E. coli (Fig. 2c, d and e). Moreover, the above selective labeling of H. pylori could not be found when using the FL488-PEG-DFO co-polymer (Fig. S6) as a comparison (Fig. S7). This contrast indicated that the targeting ability of the Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer to H. pylori was ascribed to its hemin moiety. Of note, it has been elucidated that the outer membrane receptor FrpB1 takes the key responsibility for binding heme, so that H. pylori is able to transport heme as one of the strategies for iron acquisition to support its living (Richard et al., 2019). However, the stable form of heme in vitro is its oxidative product of hemin after extraction. Interestingly, it was shown herein that hemin could also mediated a binding to H. pylori, which might be ascribed to the highly similar skeleton structure between heme and hemin. Therefore, it is proposed that the targeting of the Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer to H. pylori is attributed to the biological binding between the hemin ligand and its specific receptor of the outer membrane protein FrpB1. Simultaneously, the Hemin-PEG-DFO co-polymer was validated to be able to chelate Fe3+ (Fig. S8). Therefore, it was able to work as the previously designed bifunctional module 2.

As previously conceived, the Fe3+/CDs system could function as the transducing module 3. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence of CDs (30.0 ng/mL) could be quenched using 3.0 nM Fe3+ in 1 mL of 1 × PBS buffer, exhibiting a low and flat spectrum (blue curve) as compared to the peak-like spectrum with a strong intensity of the same concentration of bare CD solution (Fig. 3a, red curve). Additionally, the Fe3+ concentration-dependent quenching efficiency of CDs was observed (Fig. 3a, red curve to blue curve). These results indicated that Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of CDs while its partial removal enabled the fluorescence recovery of CDs. Thus, the designed function of module 3 was also validated. Interestingly, the fluorescence recovery could respond to a tiny decrease in Fe3+ concentration with an obvious increase (average by 17.2 ± 3.5%) to achieve a clearly distinguished signal amplification (Fig. 3a), indicating the sensitivity of this transducer.

Module 3, together with the preparation of modules 1 and 2, could reveal the working principle of the designed biosensor. The operating sequence of the biosensor was as follows: initially, 1.5 mg of module 1 was incubated with 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL H. pylori in 1.0 mL of 1 × PBS buffer for 60.0 min. Subsequently, the module 1-H. pylori complex was collected using magnetic separation, and it was transferred to 5.0 mg of module 2 solution in 1.0 mL of 1 × PBS buffer. After incubating for another 30.0 min, the complex of module 1-H. pylori-module 2 was again collected using magnetic separation, and it was transferred to 1 mL of module 3 solution (30.0 ng/mL CDs and 3.0 nM Fe3+ in 1 × PBS buffer). Notably, following magnetic separation, an obvious recovery of CD fluorescence was observed (Fig. 3b, red curve) as compared to that of quenched CDs (Fig. 3b, blue curve). This result clearly demonstrated the working principle of the designed biosensor. Briefly, module 1 was used to specifically enrich H. pylori from aqueous samples, and module 2 was used to chelate Fe3+ from module 3 through its DFO moiety. The scavenging of Fe3+ ions from CDs mediated via magnetic separation could actuate the “turn-on” signal of CDs, so that the consequent signal of recovered fluorescence could be read out. As different densities of H. pylori could absorb different amounts of module 2, which could correspondingly chelate different amounts of Fe3+ from module 3, a correlation could be drawn between the H. pylori concentrations and recovered fluorescence, facilitating the quantification of bacteria according to fluorescent signals.

Previously, many siderophore-based biosensors have been constructed to detect Fe3+ due to the strong chelating abilities of siderophores to iron (Nosrati et al., 2018). Moreover, CDs were also studied to detect Fe3+ in a concentration-dependent manner because Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of CDs when contacted on the surfaces (Guo et al., 2015; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018). In this study, the two mechanisms were combined for the first time. This study demonstrated that the siderophore of DFO could seize and scavenge Fe3+ from the Fe3+-quenched CDs system, which indeed actuated the re-illumination of quenched CDs.

On determination of the working principle, the optimal working conditions of this biosensor were determined to be 1.0 mg/mL of module 1, 4.0 mg/mL of module 2, and 20.0 ng/mL of CDs incorporated with 0.649 mg/mL of Fe3+ as module 3 (Fig. S9). Based on the optimal composition, the reaction time of each module with its target was investigated to primarily evaluate the time-cost of this biosensor. As shown in Fig. S9d, it took at least 30.0 min for 1.0 mg/mL of module 1 to reach its highest capture efficiency of 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL H. pylori cells, which was also confirmed by the residual cell density that no longer decreased following magnetic separation. For H. pylori with cell densities lower than 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL, the reaction time for module 1 to capture maximum H. pylori cells was set as 30.0 min to guarantee a thorough binding. Furthermore, H. pylori cells exhibited the highest binding efficiency to 4.0 mg/mL of module 2 at the 10th min (Fig. S9e), and the same concentration of module 2 was able to fully chelate Fe3+ within only 5.0 min (Fig. S9f). As the three modules were successively subjected to H. pylori cells, it took 45.0 min to complete one working cycle using the designed biosensor.

Upon the working principle and the optimal working condition for each module were unraveled, the use of this biosensor in the detection of H. pylori was set to be investigated. However, before subjecting this biosensor to H. pylori cells, it was concerning that electrostatic or hydrophobic interactions would occur between module 1 and 2 or module 1 and Fe3+ in module 3, ascribed to the static and polar properties of their chemical compositions. If positive, these interactions would cause H. pylori-independent Fe3+ scavenging that brought in interfering fluorescence recovery upon magnetic separation. To justify this issue, module 1, 2 and 3 with the above-optimized concentrations were added into the same 1 mL 1 × PBS buffer to react for 5 min (the optimal reacting time for module 3). After the subsequent magnetic separation, it was found that this operation did not cause any fluorescence recovery, as shown in Fig. 4 a that the measured fluorescence spectra after this operation (cyan curve) closely overlapped with that of unreacted module 3 as the control (black curve). This result confirms that the as-mentioned undesirable Fe3+ scavenging, which comes from the interactions between the module 1 or 2 of this biosensor and Fe3+, doesn't exist, which is able to confer an accurate judgement when confronting with H. pylori-negative samples.

Following this confirmation, the detection range and limit of detection (LOD) of the biosensing platform for H. pylori were evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, the variation in fluorescence intensity could be recognized along with the change in H. pylori cell density, and no highly overlapped curve was observed. Moreover, the recovery ratios corresponded to the cell densities of H. pylori ranging from 1.0 × 101 to 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL (Fig. 4b), which were linearly correlated to [y = 0.019x + 0.00402, R2 = 0.99154] equation, where y represents the fluorescence recovery ratio and x represents the log values of H. pylori cell density for each sample. Thus, the LOD of this biosensing approach reached as low as 1 CFU/mL. These phenomena are postulated to be attributed to the high sensitivity of the transducer in this biosensor, as described above (Fig. 3a). Previously, it has been suggested that a detection technology, based on immunomagnetic beads that capture coupling fluorescence quantum dots, could achieve a detection range of 10–106 CFU/mL and LOD of 102 CFU/mL for the whole cells of H. pylori in feces (Chen et al., 2019). In another work, an aptasensor-based detection of H. pylori exhibited an LOD of 88 CFU/mL (Wu et al., 2021). These numeric comparisons reveal that the sensitivity of the biosensor was much improved than that previously reported.

Previously, transition metal ions have been shown to serve as key switches that controlled the “off” or “on” of CDs as the transducing mechanism inside their biosensor for the detection of biomolecules. For example, Park and co-workers designed a novel “on-off” biosensor for highly sensitive detection of the alkaline phosphatase (ALP) level, it took the advantage of the quenching of CDs by Cu2+ released from the coordination compound of pyrophosphate-Cu2+ upon its hydrolysis by ALP, thus establishing a correlation between the ALP level and the quenching efficiencies (Kim et al., 2018b). In another case, the harnessing of inositol hexaphosphate (IP6) to form an insoluble complex with Fe3+, which quenched yellow-fluorescent CDs, could restored the fluorescence, facilitating an “off-on” biosensor for determining IP6 in vitro and in vivo (Cui et al., 2019). These novel works are quite different from the traditional “turn-off” sensors for direct detection of Cu2+ or Fe3+ based on the quenching effect of these transition metal ions on CDs via the photo-induced electro transfer (Huang et al., 2013), whereas they ingeniously utilized this mechanism to develop Cu2+/Fe3+-switch controlled fluorescent indicators for effective transduction and sensitive signal amplification. Once again, this mechanism contributes a sensitive fluorescent biosensor in this work, in which a transducer that uses siderophore-mediated Fe3+ scavenging to turn on the fluorescence of CDs is scarcely seen elsewhere.

Although the LOD of 1 CFU/mL of the biosensor might be the lowest value ever reported for the detection of H. pylori whole cells using a fluorometric methodology, it was theoretically calculated under the optimal buffer environment. Its application to real fecal samples still needs to be specified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H. pylori in simulated fecal samples
ce:section.ce:para: As the contents of real fecal samples might exhibit a strong fluorescence, they can lead to adverse effects on the accuracy of the prepared biosensor. To verify this issue, H. pylori cells, with the final concentration varying between 1.0 × 101 to 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL, were mixed with 300 mg of stool sample from six healthy people (three 2-year-old babies and three adults, each sample was diagnosed as H. pylori-negative using q-PCR) in 1.0 mL of 1 × PBS buffer to simulate the pathological samples. Low-speed centrifugation was performed to separate solid fecal matters from H. pylori suspension, and this was followed by applying the biosensor to the obtained H. pylori suspension. A strong fluorescence interference was observed with the biosensor, and no regular fluorescence could be obtained. This was likely due to small fecal particles that could emit fluorescence and could not be separated via centrifugation. To eliminate this interference, a centrifugal microfluidic plate was used to enrich bacterial cells from the simulated samples via centrifugation (Fig. 5 a–c). As shown in Fig. 5d (column 3), approximately 50.0% of the H. pylori cells could be enriched from each simulated sample (Fig. 5d, column 2) via absolute quantification using qPCR.

Interestingly, a non-specific binding still existed in module 1 for bacterial cells other than H. pylori in the artificially constructed bacterial community as described above (Fig. 1b). In this situation, possible electrostatic interactions between the cell membranes/walls of these non-specifically attached bacterial cells and ferric ions of module 3, could cause siderophore-independent binding of Fe3+; Moreover, affinity of heme to gastrointestinal bacteria other than H. pylori that derives from their active transport of heme, such as E. coli (Richard et al., 2019) and E. faecalis (Saillant et al., 2021), is able to cause extra binding of module 2 to these bacteria and consequent chelation of Fe3+. These factors thereby are highly likely to lead to fluorescence recovery on magnetic separation even there is no H. pylori existing in the reacting system, thus may generate an undesirable interfering background that affects the accuracy of this biosensing platform. As shown in Fig. 5d (column 4, bottom line), an average background fluorescence recovery ratio of approximately 1.7% was actually discovered from the blank fecal sample without H. pylori supplementation to the microfluidic enrichment medium following detection with the biosensor. This would lead to a false diagnosis that misjudges the H. pylori negative specimen to be positive. Therefore, the background value should be corrected by subtracting it from the value of each sample. With these corrections, the resulting detection values of H. pylori density could be calculated according to the previous equation (Fig. 5d, column 5). It could be found that 85.0–90.0% of H. pylori cells collected using microfluidics could be detected with the biosensor when the cell density was below 104, whereas over 90.0% of H. pylori cells could be detected when the cell density was above 104 (104 included). Overall, the coupling of microfluidics and biosensor enabled the reappearance of H. pylori cells in original fecal samples with ratios of approximately 50.0% against the cell densities ranging from 10-107 (Fig. 5d, column 6). Thus, the LOD obtained with the collaboration of this centrifugal microfluidic plate and biosensor can be as low as 101 CFU/mL while it cannot reach the calculated limit of 1 CFU/mL, which is still beyond the capabilities of all biosensors for H. pylori. Thus, microfluidics and biosensor together comprise an ultimate platform for the ultrasensitive detection of H. pylori in human feces. It is estimated that microfluidic enrichment takes 20 min to complete. Following addition, this biosensing platform together requires 65 min to finish one test of H. pylori from a fecal specimen. This time-cost is much improved as it has been shortened by nearly 50.0% as compared to the former method using immunomagnetic beads coupling fluorescent quantum dots (Chen et al., 2019). Although it has been claimed that an aptasensor-based platform took only 5 min to finish a naked eye detection of H. pylori, the time-cost of bacterial separation and optimal reaction conditions were not taken into consideration (Wu et al., 2021), making it difficult to compare with this work.

In this study, an accurate, sensitive, and instant antibody-independent biosensor was readily developed for detecting H. pylori in human feces. Microfluidics along with the biosensor contribute to a novel biosensing platform for H. pylori, showing the lowest detection limit of 101 CFU/mL ever reported. The use of this biosensing platform shortened the diagnosis process to 65 min, suggesting its instant operation. Moreover, the integration of aptamer and other easily available reagents to replace antibodies indicates the economic viability of the platform. These advantages indicate that this biosensing platform overcomes the shortcomings of the already used immunoassays, paving a foundation for its future practical translation in clinics to facilitate an alternative patient-friendly non-invasive diagnosis of H. pylori.
This work was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province [Grant number: 2019GSF107065].

Z.Z.W., Data curation, Methodology, Software; H.Y.W., Methodology; X.H.C., Investigation, Methodology; J.Y.G., Methodology; L.L.W. and Q.J.D., Resources; C.G.L. and Z.M.C., Supervision; Z.C., Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - the original draft, review & editing.

Zhuangzhuang Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Software. Hongying Wang: Methodology. Xiaohong Cheng: Investigation, Methodology. Jiayue Geng: Methodology. Lili Wang: Resources. Quanjiang Dong: Resources. Chenguang Liu: Supervision. Zhenming Chi: Supervision. Zhe Chi: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Professor Bo Ma from the Qingdao Institute of Biomass Energy and Bioprocess Technology for the kindly gifted microfluidic plates. We also devote our gratitude to those beloved baby stool donators, including Jinyu Chi, the baby daughter of Professor Zhe Chi, for their great efforts to eat and poop for healthy growth with high hopes.
